1.synthesis: In the seventh month the people gather “as one man” to Jerusalem, and Jeshua and Zerubbabel build the altar of the God of Israel “to offer burnt offerings on it, as it is written in the Law of Moses” (v.2). They do so “for fear was on them because of the peoples of the lands,” and resume the daily offerings and the Feast of Booths (vv.3–4), even before the temple foundation is laid (v.6). The integrative thread is the priority of restored worship grounded in Scripture: before walls or temple, the returnees rebuild the altar and resume sacrifice “as it is written,” worshiping in obedience and in faith despite their fear — putting the worship of God first and conforming it to his revealed word.
1.historical_context: The gathering “as one man” in the seventh month (v.1) coincided with the appointed feasts, especially Booths (v.4; Leviticus 23:34). Rebuilding the altar first (v.2) allowed the resumption of sacrifice on the original site even before temple construction. Their fear of the surrounding peoples (v.3) makes their obedient worship an act of faith. All was done “as it is written in the Law of Moses,” the post-exilic community’s devotion to Scripture.
1.literary_structure: This section (vv.1–6) opens the rebuilding narrative with the altar and sacrifices, the repeated “as it is written” (v.2, v.4) marking obedience to Scripture as the governing principle. Worship is restored before the temple structure, a deliberate ordering that the narrative emphasizes.
1.theological_application: The worship of God is the first priority of his restored people, established on the foundation of his written word — “as it is written” (v.2; Matthew 4:4). That they worshiped despite fear of hostile neighbors (v.3) shows faith that obeys before circumstances are safe. The believer learns to put the worship of God first, to ground it in Scripture rather than preference, and to obey even when surrounded by opposition (Acts 5:29). That the returnees rebuilt the altar and resumed sacrifice “as it is written” before laying the temple foundation (vv.2–3) shows that the worship of God, grounded in his written word, is the first priority of his people (Matthew 4:4). That they worshiped despite fear of hostile neighbors (v.3) models a faith that obeys before circumstances are safe. The believer learns to put worship first, to conform it to Scripture rather than preference, and to obey God even amid opposition (Acts 5:29).
1.christology: The rebuilt altar and resumed sacrifices (vv.2–3) point to the one altar and final sacrifice of Christ, who offered himself once for all (Hebrews 10:10) and is himself the altar from which his people partake (Hebrews 13:10). The priority of worship grounded in Scripture anticipates the worship “in spirit and truth” that the Father seeks (John 4:24), centered on the Son. The Feast of Booths, recalling God’s dwelling with his pilgrim people, looks to the Word who “tabernacled” among us (John 1:14).

7.synthesis: They give money to masons and carpenters, and food, drink, and oil to the Sidonians and Tyrians to bring cedar from Lebanon by sea to Joppa, as Cyrus had authorized (v.7). In the second year, Zerubbabel, Jeshua, and the Levites begin the work, appointing Levites from twenty years old to supervise (vv.8–9). The integrative thread is the orderly resumption of the temple-building: following the pattern of Solomon’s temple — cedar from Lebanon, Levitical oversight — the community organizes the work under its leaders, the second temple rising in continuity with the first and according to the proper order God had established.
7.historical_context: The procurement of cedar from Lebanon via Tyre and Sidon to Joppa (v.7) deliberately echoes Solomon’s temple-building (1 Kings 5:6; 2 Chronicles 2:16), establishing continuity. The appointment of Levites from twenty years old to oversee (v.8) follows David’s reorganization (1 Chronicles 23:24). The work began in the second year of the return, under Zerubbabel and Jeshua’s joint civil and priestly leadership.
7.literary_structure: This brief section (vv.7–9) records the organizational beginning of the temple work, the Solomonic parallels (v.7) underscoring continuity with the first temple. The Levitical supervision (v.9) sets up the foundation-laying ceremony that follows.
7.theological_application: God’s work proceeds through orderly organization, proper leadership, and the faithful use of resources (vv.8–9; 1 Corinthians 14:40). The continuity with Solomon’s pattern teaches respect for the God-given order of worship across generations. The believer learns that zeal for God’s work is to be joined with diligent, organized labor, each contributing according to appointment, so that the house of God is built decently and in order (Nehemiah 4:6). God’s work proceeds through orderly organization, proper leadership, and the faithful use of resources (vv.8–9), and the deliberate echo of Solomon’s temple-building (v.7; 1 Kings 5:6) teaches respect for the God-given order of worship across generations. The believer learns that zeal for God’s work is to be joined with diligent, organized labor, each contributing according to his appointment, so that the house of God is built decently and in order (1 Corinthians 14:40).
7.christology: The second temple rising in continuity with Solomon’s (v.7) points forward to the greater temple in Christ, who is greater than the temple (Matthew 12:6) and whose body is the true dwelling of God (John 2:21). The cedar brought to build God’s house anticipates the church, built of living stones into a holy temple in the Lord (Ephesians 2:21), of which Christ is the cornerstone, the whole structure joined and rising under his headship. The second temple rising in continuity with Solomon’s, built with cedar of Lebanon (v.7), points to the greater temple in Christ, who is greater than the temple (Matthew 12:6) and whose body is the true dwelling of God (John 2:21). The redeemed are built as living stones into a holy temple in the Lord (Ephesians 2:21), of which Christ is the cornerstone, the whole structure joined and rising under his headship to the praise of God (1 Peter 2:5).

10.synthesis: When the builders lay the foundation, the priests and Levites praise the LORD “according to the directions of David,” singing, “For he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever toward Israel” (vv.10–11). But the old men who had seen the first temple weep loudly even as others shout for joy, so that the sound of weeping could not be distinguished from the shout (vv.12–13). The integrative thread is joy and grief mingled at a humbler new beginning: the foundation occasions praise for God’s enduring covenant love, yet the tears of those who remembered Solomon’s glory acknowledge how diminished this temple seems — a poignant scene of hope and loss together, the restoration real but the former glory not yet recovered.
10.historical_context: The foundation-laying ceremony (v.10) followed Davidic liturgical order, with trumpets, cymbals, and the antiphonal refrain of Psalm 136:1 (“his steadfast love endures forever”). The weeping of the old men who remembered Solomon’s temple (v.12) reflects the modest scale of the second temple, a theme Haggai addresses, promising its latter glory would exceed the former (Haggai 2:3, 9).
10.literary_structure: This closing scene (vv.10–13) climaxes chapter 3 with the mingled sound of joy and weeping, an evocative image of the bittersweet restoration. The praise “according to the directions of David” (v.10) anchors the new work in continuity, while the indistinguishable shouting and weeping (v.13) capture the complex emotion of a diminished but genuine new beginning.
10.theological_application: God’s steadfast love endures forever (v.11), and his restorations, though often humbler than former glory, are real grounds for praise (Zechariah 4:10, “whoever has despised the day of small things”). The mingling of joy and grief (vv.12–13) honors honest emotion, holding gratitude for present mercy and sorrow over past loss together. The believer learns to praise God for new beginnings while not despising small things, trusting that he is faithful to bring his work to fullness. “His steadfast love endures forever” (v.11) is the ground of praise even at a humbler new beginning, and the mingling of joy and weeping (vv.12–13) honors honest emotion that holds gratitude for present mercy and grief over past loss together. The believer learns not to despise the “day of small things” (Zechariah 4:10) but to praise God for genuine restorations, trusting that he who began the work will faithfully bring it to its appointed fullness (Philippians 1:6).
10.christology: The diminished second temple that drew tears would one day be filled with a greater glory, for Haggai promised that the Desire of nations would come and the latter glory exceed the former (Haggai 2:7, 9) — fulfilled when Christ himself entered that very temple (Malachi 3:1; Luke 2:27–32). The refrain “his steadfast love endures forever” (v.11) finds its deepest ground in the covenant love revealed in Christ (Romans 8:38–39), and the “day of small things” gives way to the glory of the true temple, his risen body and his church (Ephesians 2:21–22).
